era_context: The late 14th century: Ming China consolidating Han rule; Ottoman expansion in the Balkans; Western European kingdoms in dynastic conflict; the Great Schism fragmenting papal authority; the Timurid Empire in Central Asia.

events.title: Ottoman siege of Constantinople; Byzantine crisis
events.description: The Ottoman Turks, under Bayezid I, besieged Constantinople in 1391–1392, putting intense pressure on the declining Byzantine Empire. The siege was not a full assault but rather a blockade and demonstration of Ottoman power. The Byzantine Emperor Manuel II was forced to negotiate and eventually pay tribute to the Ottomans. This siege marked a critical moment in the decline of the Byzantine Empire, which would eventually fall to the Ottomans in 1453. The siege demonstrated Ottoman military superiority and the vulnerability of Constantinople, which was now surrounded by Ottoman-controlled territory.
events.certainty_note: The Ottoman siege of Constantinople in 1391–1392 is well-documented in both Byzantine and Ottoman sources.

events.title: Manuel II becomes Byzantine Emperor
events.description: Manuel II Palaiologus became Byzantine Emperor in 1391, succeeding his father John V. Manuel II was one of the last effective Byzantine emperors, known for his diplomatic skills and his efforts to preserve the Byzantine state in the face of Ottoman expansion. He ruled during the critical period of Ottoman siege and expansion, and his reign saw the Byzantine Empire reduced to Constantinople and a few outlying territories. Manuel II was also a scholar and theologian, and he left behind important writings on Byzantine history and culture. His reign lasted until 1425, and he was succeeded by his son John VIII.
events.certainty_note: Manuel II's accession to the throne in 1391 is precisely dated in Byzantine sources.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor purges officials; Ming bureaucratic consolidation
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) was known for his periodic purges of officials he suspected of disloyalty or corruption. In the early 1390s, he continued this practice, executing or demoting numerous officials and consolidating his personal power. These purges were part of his broader strategy to eliminate potential rivals and establish absolute imperial authority. The purges were brutal and created an atmosphere of fear in the Ming bureaucracy, but they also helped to establish the centralized authority that would characterize the Ming Dynasty. The Hongwu Emperor's reign saw the transformation of the Chinese state from a decentralized feudal system to a highly centralized bureaucratic empire.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's purges were a continuous process throughout his reign; specific purges in 1391 are not well-documented.

events.title: Gian Galeazzo Visconti expands Milan's territory
events.description: Gian Galeazzo Visconti, the Duke of Milan, was engaged in military campaigns to expand Milanese territory in the early 1390s. He was consolidating his power and extending his influence across northern Italy through a combination of military conquest and diplomatic maneuvering. His expansion was part of the broader process of state-building in Renaissance Italy, where powerful city-states were competing for dominance. Gian Galeazzo's Milan would become one of the most powerful states in Italy, rivaling Venice and Florence.
key_figures: Gian Galeazzo Visconti (Duke of Milan, r. 1378–1402)
events.certainty_note: Gian Galeazzo's territorial expansion was a gradual process spanning the 1380s–1390s; specific campaigns in 1391 are not well-documented.

events.title: Great Schism continues; papal authority fragmented
events.description: The Great Schism (1378–1417) continued unabated in 1391, with rival popes in Rome and Avignon claiming legitimacy. The schism was a major crisis for the Catholic Church, fragmenting papal authority and creating confusion among the faithful. Efforts to resolve the schism through councils and diplomatic pressure were ongoing but unsuccessful. The schism would not be resolved until the Council of Constance (1414–1418), which would establish a single pope and reform the Church's governance. The schism had profound effects on European politics, as different monarchs supported different popes based on political interests.
events.certainty_note: The Great Schism and its dates are well-documented in papal and ecclesiastical records.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed above. However, the Ottoman siege of Constantinople in 1391–1392 was not a full assault but rather a blockade and demonstration of power; it is sometimes overstated in historical accounts as a more serious threat than it actually was. The Hongwu Emperor's purges were a continuous process, not discrete events in specific years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1391 is dominated by European, Islamic, and East Asian written sources. Sub-Saharan African, Pacific, and interior American events are almost entirely absent. The sources are predominantly later compilations rather than contemporary records, introducing dating uncertainty and potential bias toward court-sponsored narratives. Byzantine sources are particularly rich for this year due to the significance of Manuel II's accession and the Ottoman siege.

graph_edges.note: The Ottoman siege of Constantinople in 1391 demonstrated Ottoman power and contributed to the eventual fall of the city in 1453
Manuel II's accession in 1391 began a reign that would last until 1425
The Hongwu Emperor's purges and consolidation of power in the 1390s set the stage for succession disputes after his death
The unresolved Great Schism of 1391 led to the Council of Constance in 1414
